item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is of a phone bill that could not be reliably sourced to anywhere. As per the decision of Licensing Staff, this image is considered user-owned and thus compatible with the site's license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somebody has some PTSD from fighting with MS Excel, methinks…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, this is the kind of detail I would expect from the Foundation. A lot of articles I've read so far write the effects in a few paragraphs and throw in some [REDACTED]s to fill gaps, but this one is extremely specific about what the skip can do and what it can't do. The apocalypse angle might be a bit overplayed, but it still gets a +1 from me due to the technical breakdown.

I hadn't even thought of hazard pictographs being in use, even after reading ●●|●●●●●|●●|● but after their mention in this article it seems like something that would be widely used as a general level of precaution, even for SCPs that require a high security level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand why 1866 should cause 6:30 PM to become 1:05 AM, instead of 1:05 PM. It seems like changing the PM to AM is outside of the scope of its behavioral algorithm.

Demanding dates be written out with month name seems a little like overkill, It would make more sense to simply designate the containment and adjacent areas not available for storage of permanent records of any kind, and a secondary containment for infected items.

From a storytelling perspective, I think that the appendix belongs before the recovery log.

The only part that feels really out of place for an SCP is the invoking of "possibly a trial run by an unknown terrorist group". It doesn't fit the SCP mythos at all, especially as a one-off event that hasn't been repeated. It feels like 1866 was an unusually infectious Anomalous Item, rather than something manufactured to spread fear and panic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Additional resources are to be allocated in November 2012, in preparation for the expected outbreaks which will begin in November and continue into December.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor error I just noticed:

November 2012 (12/12)

Not sure what part to fix, though. Is it meant to say December 2012 or 11/12?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one for a number of reasons, but chiefly because it's at heart a very simple SCP that would maybe make me think 'So what?' - it's easy to contain, and doesn't seem to do too much. But then the exploration and amount of detail that went into what could happen when something as initially innocuous-seeming as this is unleashed is terrific. It makes me think again about how much chaos even all the Safe class SCPs could cause if they weren't contained.

Also, the actual way the date changes is cool. It's very mch like the kind of thing a man might do idly in his head…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God I wish we could upvote comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was right in the middle of going "Hulk no like…stupid math-words.." and then the implications of this all kind of rushed through my head like VROOOOSH and then I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It's not something that sounds that bad at first, until you go into the implications. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting idea, but I felt very bored throughout the whole article. The length didn't make it better. I really tried to find something that would spark my interest or draw me into the SCP, but all I got was the same sounding stuff. Sorry, downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic. The fact that it doesn't just alter every date it comes across, that there are times when its effects are more pronounced, is just amazing. Not to mention the realization of how much we depend upon dates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been over and over this comment in my head since you left it last night, and I can't quite figure out what you're saying is wrong with it. Any chance I could get you to elaborate on this? (If not here, then by PM, maybe?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes teachers teach concepts like simplifying fractions a little too well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how you reversed the usual order here. Typically, the effect is described and then there is some discussion of the aftermath and clean-up efforts. This comes across as saying "Look at this scary thing. It's really scary because it did all this." By putting the math last you are saying "It did all this, it's really scary. Which is even worse because it's from this tiny little math glitch that you might not think would be dangerous."

By playing with the style a little, you've managed to take a pretty subtle, even boring, anomaly and transmute it into a truly believable threat. Kudos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm stupid; I don't get it. 6__9

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely agreed. It doesn't make your walls bleed, it's not hiding under your bed, but the implication that it was created by a terrorist group who could introduce a new one at any place and time - and, presumably, bringing the global economy to a screeching halt - is actually really scary, once you read and understand the entire thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is at it's basic fundaments, boring and a bit dull. It gets my upvote for being a good story and having devastating effects. But it's far from perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I got the interesting bit, but the boring bit was very interesting so upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this one is extraordinary. The central idea, the mixture of the rational math and utter lunacy of the effect, the forensic detail…
This is the kind of SCP I aspire to write.
+vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, the execution's good, but ultimately this did nothing for me. The "data gets messed up, people start dying" trope is fairly familiar, and I really thought you were going somewhere beyond that, but…nope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this a lot. It's horrifying but not in a scary, rip-your-face-off way. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The execution is okay, but ultimately it comes across as lackluster and muddy. It's kind of like listening to a badly recorded jazz show from the 40's, you know that there's some fantastic shit happen'n on-stage, but you just can't hear it clearly.

One of the problems here is that you go too deep into what it does and not deep enough into why what it does matters. The 4. footnote was rather dull, and didn't seem to accurately portray the object's effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1193 (Which happens to be one of ophite's.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not every idea's going to come with elbows every 4 kilometers, after all

What in the fuck?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The common-divisor thing really hit me hard, since I find myself doing the same thing in my head with numbers all the time. It really made this more believable for me. Good work :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i thought it was boring at first, but the recovery log really worked for me. i thought the addendum was boring, but that doesn't mean I won't +1 this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or your employer's payroll department.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that you finally posted this! I feel like it being a phone bill really adds some kind of punch. I hope this gets all the (positive) attention this deserves. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The boring-to-oh-fuck progression was actually kind of the goal. Or, at least, it became the goal when I realized this was the only idea I was finding interesting enough to write up. (Not every idea's going to come with elbows every 4 kilometers, after all.) So I will take that "well-executed" and be damned happy about it; thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like concept but I'm not in a position to judge this SCP. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an extraordinarily well-executed kind of boring idea. Which isn't precisely a criticism: the fact that it's slightly boring is indispensable to the execution. It's, you know, "Oh, it's a phone bill which screws up dates. Oh, shit. What if dates _really_ got screwed up. Oh _fuck_, it's spreading. Oh, what the actual fuck this is horrible."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God damn it, so that's what happened to my last electricity bill!

I like this, especially considering what could happen if this got into, say, a large office building in New York. Like, say, the Stock Exchange. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, well, it's about a phone bill, and involves math. I knew going in that boring people was going to be a risk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well the Execution was good but the Idea really did bore me. I understand why this is needed to be contained but it itself is not interesting in general. Then again, It's just my opinion.
